perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with the laws of Taiwan. The Parties shall first seek to solve any dispute arising out of or related to this Agreement through negotiation. If the Parties fail to solve such dispute through negotiation, each Party shall have the right to issue notice ("Dispute Notice") to the other Party, and such Dispute Notice shall include the content of the dispute. If the Parties fail to resolve such dispute amicably through negotiation within sixty (60) days from the date on which a Party issues its Dispute Notice to the other Party, each Party shall have the right to submit such dispute to the Hong Kong International Arbitration Center, and proceed with the arbitration procedures in accordance with the Rules of the International Chamber of Commerce with three (3) arbitrators. Each Party shall each select one (1) arbitrator, and the third arbitrator shall be appointed by the two (2) arbitrators so selected. All language used in such proceedings shall be Mandarin Chinese. The Parties agree to keep the content of the dispute and the proceeding of the arbitration confidential. The arbitration award shall be final and binding on the Parties. The losing Party in such arbitration shall bear all of the costs and expenses related to the arbitration as determined by the arbitrators in such dispute (including attorney's fees).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with the laws of Taiwan. The Parties shall first seek to solve any dispute arising out of or related to this Agreement through negotiation. If the Parties fail to solve such dispute through negotiation, each Party shall have the right to issue notice ("Dispute Notice") to the other Party, and such Dispute Notice shall include the content of the dispute. If the Parties fail to resolve such dispute amicably through negotiation within sixty (60) days from the date on which a Party issues its Dispute Notice to the other Party, each Party shall have the right to submit such dispute to the Hong Kong International Arbitration Center, and proceed with the arbitration procedures. The arbitration award shall be final and binding on the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause outlining the arbitrator selection process and rules of the International Chamber of Commerce with three (3) arbitrators, the contract creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability regarding the arbitration process, which can be seen as restricting dispute resolution. This is a legal risk in Taiwan, as its Arbitration Act stipulates clear procedures for arbitration.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the details on the number of arbitrators and the rules (International Chamber of Commerce) contradicts the Taiwan Arbitration Act, which requires clarity in arbitration agreements. Article 15 addresses the selection of arbitrators, Article 25 outlines the procedures, and Article 31 specifies the details to be included in the arbitral award. The omission introduces uncertainty, potentially rendering the arbitration clause unenforceable under Taiwan law.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies the arbitrator selection process and rules. By removing this information, the changed text creates a significant ambiguity regarding the arbitration process. The Law Explanation states that this omission contradicts the Taiwan Arbitration Act, which requires clarity in arbitration agreements. While the scraped law snippets are missing, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight that the omission introduces uncertainty, potentially rendering the arbitration clause unenforceable under Taiwan law. Therefore, the removal of this clause directly contradicts the need for a clear and enforceable arbitration process, as it omits legally important information, especially regarding dispute resolution.
